items.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
items.summary: Moscow Times opinion piece, Ilya Klishin argues that a small factual error in foreign reporting around Putin-Macron talks exposed a larger expertise problem in commentary about Russia.
items.context: Why it matters: it gives a dated reference point for media analysis and links to The Moscow Times.

items.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
items.summary: Moscow Times article revisits the 2011-2012 fair-elections protests a decade later and asks whether the movement truly "failed" or instead reshaped the political field in slower ways.
items.context: Why it matters: it helps compare current claims on civic protests with the original text.

items.title: TEDx speaker profile: Ilya Klishin
items.summary: TEDxChisinau documentation works as a verifiable event record and speaker-profile source rather than a long-form analytical text. It provides structured metadata that supports biography and timeline consistency: location (Chisinau), event date (September 23, 2012), venue details, and program framing under the theme "Next Level.
items.context: Why it matters: it helps compare current claims on public speaking with the original text.

items.title: TV Rain launched "Our Soldiers" civilian investigation
items.summary: This August 2014 TV Rain project page introduces "Our Soldiers," a public-facing investigation into reports of Russian servicemen killed, missing, or captured near the Ukrainian border during a period of official denials. The framing is explicit: the goal is not rhetorical escalation but factual clarification of identities, numbers, and circumstances.
items.context: Why it matters: it explains war-reporting verification with direct access to the primary source.

items.title: Freedom House referenced TV Rain reporting on Russian soldiers in eastern Ukraine
items.summary: Freedom House’s Nations in Transit 2015 report on Russia provides institutional documentation of political repression, media pressure, and war-related information control during 2014. For this digest context, the key section is Independent Media and related civil-society analysis.
items.context: Why it matters: it places institutional citation in a concrete editorial moment (2015).

items.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
items.summary: A February 2022 column on how a seemingly minor factual error in diplomatic coverage can expose a deeper expertise problem in Russia reporting.
items.context: It shows a practical standard for distinguishing evidence-based analysis from rumor-driven framing during a high-risk period.
items.section_intro: Public-interest reporting and commentary tied to concrete events and timelines.

items.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
items.summary: A retrospective on the 2011-2012 protest cycle and the long-term political effects that can remain invisible in short-horizon news judgments.
items.context: It provides context on civic mobilization beyond immediate outcomes and helps interpret protest narratives with more historical precision.
items.section_intro: Public-interest reporting and commentary tied to concrete events and timelines.

items.title: Commentary on pressure against independent media institutions
items.summary: A structural reading of how sustained pressure campaigns weaken independent newsrooms step by step rather than through one isolated action.
items.context: It frames media pressure as a process with social consequences, useful for readers evaluating institutional risk and resilience.
items.section_intro: Work on institutions, platform pressure, and editorial decision environments.

items.title: Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia
items.summary: A scenario-based piece that maps likely technical and regulatory pathways for restricting major social platforms.
items.context: It translates abstract censorship risk into concrete operational scenarios for journalists, media managers, and platform observers.
items.section_intro: Work on institutions, platform pressure, and editorial decision environments.

items.title: Early warning analysis on Russian troll expansion
items.summary: A 2014 analysis arguing that domestic trolling practices were likely to scale outward into external influence operations.
items.context: This text captures an early analytical frame that later became central in wider Western debates on disinformation.
items.section_intro: Early and later-stage analysis of networked manipulation patterns.

items.title: Warning! An Army of Russian Trolls Has Invaded TikTok
items.summary: A platform-focused follow-up that tracks how influence tactics adapt to new social formats and audience behavior.
items.context: It connects legacy troll-network logic to contemporary short-video ecosystems, useful for understanding cross-platform evolution.
items.section_intro: Early and later-stage analysis of networked manipulation patterns.

items.title: Volna Media project overview
items.summary: A practical editorial initiative for Russian-speaking emigrant and refugee audiences across multiple European countries.
items.context: It demonstrates applied media strategy under displacement conditions, where trust, local relevance, and distribution resilience are critical.
items.section_intro: Work on media products and audience needs in post-2022 exile environments.

items.title: Interview on creating an emigrant-focused media project
items.summary: An interview discussing editorial rationale, product design, and practical constraints in building media for dispersed diaspora communities.
items.context: It documents direct first-person context on strategy choices behind Volna’s audience model and rollout.
items.section_intro: Work on media products and audience needs in post-2022 exile environments.

items.title: Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King
items.summary: A commentary on symbolic conflict, cultural hierarchy, and the tension between classical authority and social-media attention dynamics.
items.context: It illustrates how cultural rhetoric can be used as political signaling in public discourse.
items.section_intro: Texts on culture as a field where political meaning and identity framing are contested.

items.title: Why Russians Dislike Good Russians in American Films
items.summary: An essay on representation politics in cinema, focusing on how “positive” portrayals can still trigger rejection in domestic interpretation.
items.context: It helps contextualize cultural narratives around image, identity, and external framing.
items.section_intro: Texts on culture as a field where political meaning and identity framing are contested.
